summary: Gruber explains how he got the Google Voice/App Store story wrong by reporting that AT&T was behind the rejection of Google Voice apps for iPhone. His source had misinterpreted information from someone with direct knowledge of Apple's decision, who said Apple didn't reject the app — meaning Apple considered it still under review, not that AT&T was responsible. Both Apple and AT&T told the FCC that AT&T played no role in the decision. Gruber traces the error to an ambiguity in emphasis: 'We didn't reject it' versus 'We didn't reject it.' He acknowledges the mistake while noting Apple's distinction between 'rejected' and 'not yet published' is semantic hair-splitting from a user's perspective.
key_points: Gruber's source misinterpreted Apple's internal position as implicating AT&T, when Apple was actually saying the app hadn't been rejected but was still under review
The error hinged on a subtle difference in emphasis: whether Apple was denying involvement (implying AT&T did it) or denying that a rejection had occurred at all
Both Apple and AT&T formally told the FCC that AT&T had no role in the Google Voice decision
Gruber acknowledges that from a user's perspective, there is no practical difference between a rejected app and one that is perpetually 'under study'
money_quotes: So either my source lied to me, or he was wrong.
My source did not (and would have no reason to) lie to me. He was wrong.
It's about the difference between: 'We didn't reject it.' and: 'We didn't reject it.'

I agree with many of you that this is semantic hair-splitting on Apple's part, insofar as from an iPhone user's perspective, there is no difference between a rejected iPhone app and a not-published iPhone app.
Contrary to published reports, Apple has not rejected the Google Voice application, and continues to study it.
My source interpreted this as meaning that if Apple did not reject it, AT&T must have, because if not AT&T, who else?
key_insight: A misinterpreted emphasis in a single sentence — 'We didn't reject it' — cascaded into a widely reported false narrative about AT&T's role, illustrating how easily sourced journalism can go wrong even without bad faith.
